However, based on the opening sections, the work has several significant weaknesses that limit confidence in its technical contribution and empirical validity. The framing is clear at a high level, but many key methodological details are either underspecified or potentially problematic: the student simulator is fit on an extremely small and narrow dataset from only 8 children in one village, raising serious concerns about generalization; the reward is defined as estimated knowledge gain from the same model used to simulate students, which risks circular evaluation; and the paper does not yet establish that the simulated environment is accurate enough to support meaningful RL training. The motivation for deep RL, and specifically PPO, is also not fully justified relative to simpler planning, bandit, or heuristic optimization baselines, especially given the limited state/action description provided so far. Clarity is uneven: the high-level pipeline is understandable, but the exposition contains grammatical issues, formatting problems, and occasional imprecision in mathematical definitions, which make the contribution feel less polished. Overall, the paper has an interesting application and potentially useful framework, but its main challenge is demonstrating that learned policies are valid improvements for real students rather than artifacts of a small, model-based simulator; stronger validation of the student model, clearer specification of the MDP, meaningful baselines, and more rigorous evaluation would be essential.

comments.title: Reward described as learning gain may conflate learning with Bayesian belief revision
comments.quote: Reward _rt_ ( _st, at_ ): Defined as the average difference between prior and posterior knowledge states based on the simulated student’s response to the tutor action _at_ to the student simulator.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as the natural objective for an ITS: reward the agent when the student model’s estimated mastery increases. The later context confirms that the authors intentionally use posterior-minus-prior knowledge probabilities as the reward, so this is not a notation accident. What remains problematic is that in BKT/HOT-DINA, the posterior change after an observed response is partly an inference update about what the student already knew, not necessarily learning caused by the activity. A correct response can raise the estimated probability of prior knowledge, while an incorrect response can lower it; only the transition/learn component corresponds directly to acquisition. If the reward is computed after sampling a response, the agent may be rewarded for evidence that reveals knowledge rather than for causing learning. If instead the intended reward is the expected posterior gain under the model, that should be stated and the response-update versus learning-transition components should be distinguished. Because this reward is central to training and evaluation, the ambiguity affects interpretation of the learned policy’s objective.

comments.title: Tutor transition is underspecified for multi-step, partially completed activities
comments.quote:
After selecting an activity, the child may complete all or part of it before selecting the next activity. RoboTutor has 1710 learning activities, each of which gives assisted practice of one or more skills on a sequence of items, such as letters or words to write, number problems to solve, or sentences to read. Each item requires one or more steps. Each step may take one or more attempts.

The simulated tutor state identifies the current content area and the child’s position in it. RoboTutor (actual or simulated) updates the position in the content area based on the percentage of correct attempts to perform the steps in an activity.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that the tutor simulator must reproduce RoboTutor’s state transitions, and they explain that transitions depend on the percentage of correct attempts. I checked the surrounding context to see whether the activity-level simulation later resolves this. Later text indicates that the student simulator estimates whether an activity is correct or wrong, but the paper does not specify how that binary or probabilistic activity outcome is converted into the percentage of correct attempts required by the BACK/SAME/NEXT/SKIP rule. Because activities can contain varying numbers of items, steps, and attempts, and because children may complete only part of an activity, this mapping is not a trivial detail. It affects both the tutor-state transition and the distribution of future activities. To make the simulator reproducible, the paper needs to specify how many attempts are simulated per activity, how partial completion is handled, and how correctness percentages are computed from the HOT-DINA response model.

comments.title: Intervention point is ambiguous because RoboTutor choices are described as child-selected
comments.quote: It lets the child select among doing the activity at that position, advancing to the next activity, repeating the same activity (from the previous content area), or exiting RoboTutor.
comments.explanation: At first this reads as useful background about the deployed RoboTutor interface. However, the RL formulation says the agent’s action corresponds to a tutor decision, while this sentence says that in the logged system the child selects among activity options, including exiting. I looked for contextual resolution: later sections define agent types that choose thresholds, promote/demote decisions, or activities, and the conclusion acknowledges that the experiments do not predict children backing out. Still, the passage leaves unclear how the simulator treats child agency during training and evaluation. Does the learned policy replace the child’s choice entirely, constrain the menu shown to the child, or model the probability that the child accepts/exits? This matters because historical data include self-selection and disengagement behavior, while the simulated policy appears to control sequencing directly. Without specifying the intervention point, a reader could misinterpret the learned policy as deployable when it may only be optimizing an idealized setting with no student choice or dropout.

comments.title: Threshold rule lacks numerical and boundary details needed to reproduce the tutor simulator
comments.quote:
Specifically, it uses fixed heuristic thresholds (called

LOW, MID, HI) on this percentage to demote BACK to the previous position, stay at the SAME position, promote to the NEXT position, or SKIP to the position thereafter.
comments.explanation: The qualitative description of the threshold mechanism is clear, and Figure 2 may illustrate the intended ordering of decisions. But the passage does not give the actual LOW, MID, and HI values, nor does it specify boundary conventions such as whether equality at a threshold maps to the lower or upper action. These details are not cosmetic: for Type 1 policies and for reproducing the historical RoboTutor baseline, small differences in thresholds can change curriculum position transitions and therefore the sequence of future activities. If the figure contains only a schematic, the method remains underdetermined. The paper should state the numeric thresholds and comparison rules explicitly, or explain how they are estimated or initialized.

comments.title: Bayesian fitting procedure is not specified enough to reproduce the HOT-DINA parameters
comments.quote: We use MCMC sampling for Bayesian inference with PyStan rather than the OpenBUGS Gibbs sampling package used in the original HOT-DINA work because PyStan is faster and handles larger datasets. Nevertheless, fitting the 118-parameter HOTDINA model to 42,010 attempts took approximately 4 days on a supercomputer with 128 GB and 28 cores.
comments.explanation: The authors provide useful high-level information about the model size, data size, software, and compute cost, and the later table reports posterior summaries for some parameters. However, for a Bayesian model, saying that PyStan and MCMC were used is not sufficient to reproduce the fitted student simulator. The paper does not specify priors, parameter constraints or transformations, number of chains, warmup and sampling iterations, target acceptance settings, convergence diagnostics such as \(\hat R\) or effective sample size, or how posterior samples were reduced to simulator parameters. Since the learned policy and reported gains depend directly on these fitted parameters, these omissions are methodological rather than merely presentational.

comments.title: Abstract describes the policy as maximizing immediate reward rather than expected return
comments.quote: A learned policy represents a mapping from each state to an action that maximizes the reward, i.e. the upward distance to the next state in the multi-dimensional space.
comments.explanation: The introduction later states the standard RL objective correctly: maximize long-term expected reward. So this abstract sentence is partly resolved by context and may be intended as an informal simplification. Still, as written it suggests that the learned policy greedily chooses the action with the largest one-step increase in the knowledge vector. In RL, including PPO as used here, the policy is optimized for expected return, not necessarily immediate reward; an action with lower immediate gain could be preferable if it leads to better future states. Because the paper later compares local and global impacts, preserving this distinction is important. A small rephrasing to 'maximizes expected cumulative reward' would avoid the misleading implication.

comments.title: Binary latent variables are conflated with knowledge probabilities
comments.quote: It should be noted that variables _α, y, and Y_ are all binary, ie., they take on value of either 1 or 0.
comments.explanation: I first read this as standard HOT-DINA/DINA notation: α, y, and Y may be binary latent or observed indicators, while the model works with posterior probabilities such as Pr(α_{nk}^{(t)}=1). That interpretation is supported by the surrounding definitions, which repeatedly say “Probability that student n knows skill k” and by the later state definition as a vector of estimated probabilities. However, the passage does not consistently distinguish the binary variables from their probabilities. In particular, the state and reward are defined over probabilities, not over 0/1 sampled α values. If a reader takes the sentence literally, it conflicts with the use of α as a real-valued knowledge probability in the RL state and reward. The fix is likely just notational—write Pr(α_{nk}^{(t)}=1), Pr(y_{nk}^{(t)}=1), and Pr(Y_{nj}^{(t)}=1) when referring to probabilities—but as written it leaves the simulator’s state representation ambiguous.

comments.title: Activity-level binary simulation is underspecified relative to attempt-level logs and threshold updates
comments.quote: given an activity, we should be able to simulate whether a student gets an activity right or wrong. Based on this response, we should be able to perform knowledge tracing over multiple skills to update the student’s knowledge probabilities.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to give an intuitive description of the student simulator, and it is reasonable that the omitted equations may define a probability of correctness for an activity with one or more skills. What remains unclear is the grain size. Earlier, RoboTutor activities are described as containing many items, steps, and attempts, and the tutor simulator’s BACK/SAME/NEXT/SKIP decisions depend on the percentage of correct attempts in an activity. Later, the reward baseline is reported per attempt over the first 100 attempts. In this passage, however, the simulator appears to sample a single binary “activity right or wrong” outcome and then update knowledge once. If that binary outcome is also used to drive percentage-correct thresholds, the tutor simulator effectively sees only 0% or 100% correctness unless additional aggregation is performed. If instead “activity” here really means an item/attempt, then the action space descriptions that choose among activities become misleading. The paper needs to specify how many item/step attempts are simulated per selected activity, how their correctness values are aggregated for tutor transitions, and how multi-skill activities update each skill.

comments.title: Student index changes from n to i in the reward definition
comments.quote: As the reward function for student n, we use the knowledge gain as estimated by the student model, i.e. posterior minus prior estimates of Pr(student i knows skill k), averaged over all skills.
comments.explanation: The intended meaning is mostly recoverable: the reward for student n should average posterior-minus-prior Pr(student n knows skill k) over skills k. I checked the surrounding notation, and θ_n, α_{nk}, and the later sentence all refer to student n. The isolated use of “student i” is therefore probably a typo rather than a conceptual change. Still, because i is also commonly used as a skill or item index in educational data mining, this could confuse the formal reward definition. It should be corrected to student n, or the indices should be explicitly defined if i has some other intended meaning.

comments.title: Continuous threshold action lacks ordering and feasibility constraints
comments.quote: Type 1: 3 threshold actions (LOW, MID, HI), each action _∈_ (0.0, 1.0)
comments.explanation: The intended Type 1 agent is clear: preserve RoboTutor’s heuristic structure but learn the LOW, MID, and HI cutoffs. However, RoboTutor’s threshold policy only has the intended semantics if LOW ≤ MID ≤ HI, with appropriate behavior at equality and at the 0/1 boundaries. The passage says each threshold is independently in (0, 1), but does not state how PPO enforces the ordering constraint or what happens if the policy proposes LOW > MID, MID > HI, or thresholds that make some decisions unreachable. A standard implementation would parameterize ordered thresholds, sort them before use, or add constraints/penalties; these choices can change the learned policy and its interpretation. Without this detail, the Type 1 action space and tutor transition dynamics are not reproducible.

comments.title: Tutor-state transitions for less constrained agents are not fully specified
comments.quote:
Type 3 can jump to any activity within the current content area

- Type 4 can jump to any activity in any content area; area rotation constraint is removed
comments.explanation: The hierarchy of agent types is well motivated, and the action ranges are easy to understand at a high level. What I could not resolve from the surrounding context is how the tutor state evolves after these nonstandard actions. For Type 3, if the agent jumps to an arbitrary activity in the current content area, does the curricular position become that activity’s index, does it remain unchanged, or is it updated afterward using BACK/SAME/NEXT/SKIP? For Type 4, when the area-rotation constraint is removed, is there still a current content area and curricular position after the chosen activity, and how are boundaries handled? Similar boundary questions arise for Type 2 when BACK is chosen at the first activity or SKIP near the end. These are not cosmetic details; they define the MDP transition function and therefore affect PPO training and the reported global impact.

comments.title: PPO training procedure is too underspecified to reproduce
comments.quote: In order to save computational time, we learn policy for episodes of 100 timesteps using PPO after which the episode terminates.
comments.explanation: The paper correctly identifies PPO as the optimization method and states the horizon, which are important choices. However, PPO performance is highly sensitive to implementation details: policy/value network architecture, observation normalization, action distributions for continuous versus discrete agents, learning rate, batch size, rollout length, clipping parameter, entropy bonus, value loss coefficient, number of epochs per update, number of training timesteps, random seeds, and how the eight students are sampled across episodes. None of these are specified in the passage or surrounding context. Because the main empirical claims are comparisons among learned policies, these omissions make it difficult for a reader to reproduce or assess whether the reported improvements are due to the action-space design rather than PPO tuning or training variance.

comments.title: Discounted-return statement says rewards vanish rather than discounted contributions
comments.quote: it is trivial to extend this approach to any finite number of steps or even to infinite-horizon discounted returns with discount factor _γ ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) so the rewards vanish at large timesteps.
comments.explanation: The authors are invoking a standard RL fact: with bounded rewards and γ < 1, the discounted sum can be finite because γ^t r_t goes to zero. The wording, however, says “the rewards vanish,” which is not generally true; the rewards themselves need not go to zero merely because a discount factor is used. In this educational setting rewards may eventually shrink as knowledge probabilities saturate, but that is a property of the simulator, not of discounting alone. The statement should say that the discounted contribution γ^t r_t vanishes, assuming bounded rewards.

comments.title: Local-impact explanation overstates reward independence
comments.quote: This is because successive attempts have independent rewards since the Prior(Know at step _t_ ) does not depend on the policy-proposed-action at step _t −_ 1 for local impacts.
comments.explanation: The authors’ qualitative explanation is plausible: a local counterfactual evaluation can repeatedly evaluate one-step replacements from historical states, so it can overestimate gains that would not remain available after the policy changes the student state. But calling successive attempts’ rewards “independent” is technically misleading. In the actual simulated MDP, rewards are path-dependent because each posterior knowledge state becomes the next prior. What is independent, or more precisely held fixed, is the historical prior used for each one-step local replacement. This distinction matters because the contrast between local and global impact is not statistical independence of rewards but a difference between one-step counterfactual evaluation and rolling out the learned policy with updated states.

comments.title: PPO is claimed to be better without comparative evidence
comments.quote: We used Proximal Policy Optimization because it learns better than previous reinforcement learning methods applied previously to ITS.
comments.explanation: The paper clearly does use PPO, and PPO is a well-regarded policy-gradient method with empirical advantages over some older deep RL algorithms in many domains. I also checked the related-work paragraph, where the intended contrast appears to be with Shen et al. (2018), which used DQN. What remains problematic is the stronger claim that PPO "learns better" than previous RL methods in ITS. The paper does not report an ablation or head-to-head comparison against DQN, policy gradient, bandit, or POMDP baselines on the same simulator, nor is PPO known as a uniformly superior method by standard RL theory. This should be phrased as a methodological choice, or supported with experimental comparison.

comments.title: HOT-DINA accuracy claim is broader than the cited evidence supports
comments.quote: We trained the student model using HOT-DINA because it is more accurate than other knowledge tracing methods.
comments.explanation: Earlier context does justify HOT-DINA as a plausible choice: the authors cite Xu & Mostow (2014), and they explain that HOT-DINA can extrapolate via skill difficulty, discrimination, and student proficiency. However, the evidence summarized in the paper is specifically that HOT-DINA had higher predictive accuracy than standard BKT in prior work. The conclusion broadens this to "other knowledge tracing methods," which could include many modern KT variants not evaluated here, and the paper does not report predictive validation on the RoboTutor subset. The claim would be safer as "more accurate than standard BKT in prior work" or as an empirical result if the authors add a validation comparison.

comments.title: Evaluation is described as being on historical data, but it is primarily model-based simulation
comments.quote: This paper contributes a novel framework for optimizing instructional sequencing in an intelligent tutoring system by combining knowledge tracing with deep reinforcement learning and evaluating the learned decision policy on historical data.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: the simulator is fitted from real RoboTutor logs, and local impact is defined by replacing historical activity choices. The limitation paragraph also acknowledges extrapolation from historical data. Still, the phrase "evaluating the learned decision policy on historical data" can mislead a reader into thinking the policy was evaluated directly by logged outcomes or by a standard off-policy evaluation method. From the earlier sections, both local and global impacts are expected rewards under the HOT-DINA-based simulator, with global evaluation following the learned policy from the first attempt. This distinction matters because the learned policy may choose actions not observed in the logs, and the validity then depends heavily on the simulator rather than on historical outcomes alone.

comments.title: Claim that earlier work used synthetic data is overgeneralized
comments.quote: We fit a simulated student on authentic log data from real children using RoboTutor in Tanzania, in contrast to earlier work that used synthetic data.
comments.explanation: The authors are right to emphasize that using authentic RoboTutor logs from children in Tanzania is a strength, especially compared with work that relies entirely on synthetic learners or simulated classroom data. I checked the related-work table and references, though, and the statement as written appears too broad. Several cited lines of work in knowledge tracing and instructional sequencing have used real educational log data or classroom data, even if their policy evaluation sometimes involved simulation. Unless the authors mean a narrower subset of prior work, the contrast with "earlier work" risks overstating the novelty. A more precise claim would identify which prior studies used synthetic data and how this work differs.

comments.title: Removal of the 10-item activity constraint is methodologically important but underspecified
comments.quote: We also do not make predictions on kids backing out while doing activities and remove the 10-item per activity constraint while performing our experiments.
comments.explanation: It is useful that the authors acknowledge this as a limitation. However, I looked back at the tutor-simulator description: activities contain items, steps, and attempts, and children may complete all or part of an activity before choosing another activity. The paper does not otherwise define the "10-item per activity constraint" or explain exactly how removing it changes simulated attempts, rewards, activity completion, or the 100-step horizon. Because rewards are computed per simulated attempt and policies choose activities, this implementation detail can affect both training and evaluation. The limitation is therefore not just incidental; it needs enough methodological detail for a reader to understand and reproduce the simulator.

comments.title: Related-work table mixes student models, policy classes, and optimization algorithms
comments.quote: Table 4 summarizes work that used BKT for student modeling or Deep RL for optimization.
comments.explanation: The table is useful as a compact overview, and the surrounding prose makes clear that the authors are trying to contrast both student-modeling choices and adaptive sequencing approaches. Still, the categories are somewhat inconsistent. For example, "BKT" and "HOT-DINA" are student models, while "POMDP" is usually a decision-process formulation rather than a student model in the same sense. Similarly, "Threshold," "Mastery," and "Inc Time" describe policy styles or heuristics, whereas "DQN," "PolicyGradient," and "PPO" are optimization or learning algorithms. This does not invalidate the comparison, but it can mislead readers into interpreting unlike categories as directly comparable entries.
